text: The surgeon begins a laparoscopic cholecystectomy on a 51-year-old female patient by marking the Ruvius sulcus and making an incision over the peritoneum along an oblique anterior and posterior line of dissection.

After dividing the peritoneum, the surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to lower fibro-fatty tissue from the gallbladder medially and inferiorly towards the common bile duct (CBD). The surgeon carefully manipulates a small bunch of tissue, hooking it to the gallbladder and applying a short pulse to push it medially, avoiding cautery near the biliary tree. The cystic artery, lymph node over the cystic artery, and cystic duct are visible. The surgeon identifies a branch of the cystic artery to the cystic duct and hooks fibro-fatty tissue to the gallbladder, applying a short buzz to create a window.

The surgeon lowers the fibro fatty tissue posteriorly, hugging it to the gallbladder and pushing it inferiorly and medially towards the common bile duct (CBD). A visible artery to the cystic duct is identified and buzzed to facilitate dissection in that area.

The surgeon encounters a large stone in the infundibulum, obstructing the dissection area. The stone is milked out and pushed into the gallbladder. Around the cystic artery, fibrofatty tissue is lowered using short bursts of cautery, creating a window. Bleeding occurs from a small branch of the cystic artery due to improper cautery application. Manual compression and bipolar cautery are used to control the bleeding, ensuring it is not from a major vessel. The procedure is performed above the Rouviere's sulcus.

The surgeon completes the dissection around the cystic duct, removing fibro fatty tissue to facilitate clipping. The cystic duct is skeletonized, revealing its convexity, and ensuring no traction is applied to avoid damaging medial structures. The critical view of safety is achieved, with the cystic artery clipped and the cystic duct prepared for clipping. Two clips are placed on the medial side and one on the specimen side to prevent spillage.

The surgeon gently removes the gallbladder from the gallbladder pad, addressing any minor bleeding points. The gallbladder is decompressed and detached from the liver bed, followed by thorough lavage. The gallbladder is retrieved through the epigastric port, and a small French drain is placed into the subhepatic space to manage spillage.
